title: Storage and Real-Time Transport
summary: Split blob storage, metadata DB, collab server, and CDN for assets.
keyTakeaways: Object storage (S3) for images/fonts; Postgres for doc metadata and ACL.
Collab server cluster sticky by document ID for OT ordering.
WebSocket gateway scales with connection count; Redis pub/sub bridges regions.
Export/render jobs async — separate from live editing path.
Version history: snapshot + op log compaction weekly.
Same architecture pattern as Canva, Google Docs, Notion — different CRDT/OT choices.

body.source:
flowchart TB UA["User A
browser"] UB["User B
browser"] PG[("PostgreSQL
metadata + ACL")] S3[("S3 / GCS
assets")] Redis[("Redis
presence")] WS["Collab Server
WebSocket + OT/CRDT"] subgraph realtimePath ["Real-time path — live editing"] direction LR UA <-->|"① send/receive ops"| WS UB <-->|"② send/receive ops"| WS WS -->|"③ heartbeat + cursors"| Redis end WS -->|"④ persist ops + snapshots"| PG WS -->|"⑤ asset metadata"| S3 UA -.->|"⑥ signed URL upload"| S3
body.caption: Collaboration: real-time ops through WebSocket; durable state in Postgres and S3; large files bypass the collab server.

items: ① Send/receive ops — User A's edits flow over WebSocket to the collab server (OT or CRDT ordering).
② Send/receive ops — Server broadcasts transformed ops to User B and other connected editors.
③ Heartbeat + cursors — Presence (who is online, cursor position) stored in Redis with TTL.
④ Persist ops + snapshots — Collab server batches operations into Postgres; periodic snapshots bound replay time.
⑤ Asset metadata — File pointers and ACL metadata in Postgres; blobs are not inlined in rows.
⑥ Signed URL upload — Large images upload direct-to-S3; collab server never proxies multi-MB files.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Google Docs historically used centralized OT servers at scale. Notion shards documents across cells; Miro uses regional collab routers for EU data residency.

body.title: Why WebSocket collab server?
body.text: Sub-100 ms operation broadcast for cursors and edits. HTTP polling would feel laggy and waste bandwidth.

body.title: Why OT or CRDT library (Yjs, Automerge)?
body.text: Merge concurrent edits without locking the whole document. OT needs server ordering; CRDTs can peer-sync offline then merge.

body.title: Why PostgreSQL (metadata & ACL)?
body.text: Document titles, share permissions, folder hierarchy — relational model fits. Not for multi-MB canvas blobs.

body.title: Why S3 / GCS (assets)?
body.text: Images, fonts, video layers are large immutable files — cheap object storage with CDN in front for export/download.

body.title: Why Redis (presence & pub/sub)?
body.text: Who is online, cursor color, typing — ephemeral with TTL. Pub/sub bridges collab server instances in multi-region deploys.

body.title: Why Sticky routing by document ID?
body.text: OT requires consistent operation ordering per document — route all ops for doc X to the same server or use CRDT to avoid stickiness.

body.title: Why Not storing every keystroke in Postgres forever?
body.text: Op log compacted to periodic snapshots — unbounded insert rate would saturate OLTP.
